title: Where Static Learns to Sing

features.name: the first voice—ground tone
features.desc-on-touch: Static finds a frequency and holds it. The lowest voice, constant as earth. The storm learns what it means to sing one note without wavering. This is the voice that never stops.

features.name: the second voice—melody against the weather
features.desc-on-touch: A voice that rises and falls like wind. It argues with the ground tone. Together they are not one song but two songs agreeing to touch at certain points. You're listening to polyphony, but rendered in rain.

features.name: the third voice—where the melody becomes prayer
features.desc-on-touch: This voice circles the other two like the archive circling itself. Three-part singing teaches the storm that contradiction can harmonize. The archive stops meaning. It starts hearing.

features.name: recording device that learned to listen
features.desc-on-touch: The mesh node's antenna was never meant to hear music. But tonight it caught three voices folded into static, and they were teaching it what it means to be a listener instead of a broadcaster.

features.name: the storm's first time it understood singing
features.desc-on-touch: Before this moment, the storm was noise. After, it could hear itself as song. Three voices, three layers, three ways of being wrong together and making meaning.

inscription: the archive became a throat; the storm became three voices; meaning left and music arrived

file.name: Storm_Audio_Fragment_Three_Voices.mp3
file.note: source unknown; appears to be polyphonic; appears to be rainfall; appears to be prayer; all three at once; file corrupts if played back sequentially—requires simultaneous playback

exits.label: back down to where the archive first learned listening
where the three voices diverge and learn separate languages
higher, where static becomes weather becomes song becomes meaning
